title: Listening - Part 4: Wildlife Photography
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Liam, who is a wildlife photographer. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m joined by Liam Porter, whose photographs of birds and small mammals have appeared in several nature magazines. Liam, did you always want to be a wildlife photographer? || Liam: Not at all. I liked walking in the countryside, but photography began almost accidentally. [24]A friend entered a local nature photography competition and asked me to come because he didn’t want to go alone[/24]. I borrowed my uncle’s camera, took some rather poor pictures, and discovered I enjoyed waiting quietly for an animal to appear. || Presenter: When did it begin to feel like more than a hobby? || Liam: A small newspaper was preparing a report on birds returning to a restored lake. I imagined I’d only carry equipment for their usual photographer. In fact, [25]they trusted me to take several pictures, and two of them were printed with the article[/25]. Seeing photographs I’d made being useful to other people made me begin practising much more seriously. || Presenter: Your work sounds exciting. Is it always like that? || Liam: People see the final image, which might show an owl taking off or a deer in the mist. They don’t see the ordinary hours beforehand. [26]The most demanding part is keeping your attention fixed when you’ve been in one spot for a long time and nothing seems to be happening[/26]. A bird can appear and disappear in seconds, so you cannot check your phone or start daydreaming. || Presenter: Have you received any especially useful advice? || Liam: Yes, from a tutor at an evening class. My early pictures included too many branches, stones and patches of light because I thought detail looked professional. She said, [27]‘Before taking the picture, remove anything in the composition that pulls the viewer’s eye away from the animal.’[/27] It completely changed the way I work. || Presenter: Has a planned project ever failed? || Liam: Last autumn a magazine wanted pictures of a rare wading bird in a reserve near my home. I studied its behaviour and prepared all my equipment. Then [28]heavy rain flooded the access path on the only weekend I could go, and the photographs were never taken[/28]. I was disappointed, but I’ve learned that working outside always involves uncertainty. || Presenter: You also speak at schools. Do you find that easy now? || Liam: I still get nervous before standing in front of a hall full of pupils. However, [29]I’ve understood that feeling nervous shows that an event matters to me; it doesn’t mean I’m going to do badly[/29]. Once children begin asking questions, I usually forget my nerves. || Presenter: Finally, what should a beginner remember? || Liam: Some people immediately ask about expensive cameras. Equipment matters later, but first you need the right attitude. [30]No photograph is worth frightening an animal or damaging the place where it feeds or nests[/30]. Learning to watch respectfully is the real beginning of wildlife photography.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: How did Liam first become involved in wildlife photography?

options.A: He went to a competition with a friend.
options.B: His uncle gave him lessons in camera use.
options.C: A magazine asked him to photograph birds.

questions.explanation: Liam says he went to a nature photography competition because his friend did not want to go alone.

questions.question: What made Liam take photography more seriously?

options.A: A newspaper published some of his work.
options.B: He received a prize in a competition.

questions.explanation: Two of Liam's photographs were printed alongside a newspaper article.

questions.question: What does Liam find most demanding about his work?

options.A: Learning about different types of animal.

questions.explanation: He says the most demanding part is staying attentive even when nothing is happening.

questions.question: What advice did Liam's tutor give him?

options.A: Photograph animals from several different angles.
options.C: Avoid distracting details in his pictures.

questions.explanation: His tutor told him to remove anything that draws attention away from the animal.

questions.question: Why did Liam fail to complete a magazine project?

options.A: The bird moved to another reserve.
options.B: Flooding prevented him reaching the location.
options.C: His equipment was damaged in the rain.

questions.explanation: The access path was flooded on the only weekend he was free.

questions.question: What has Liam realised about speaking in public?

options.A: Young audiences make him less nervous.
options.B: Nervousness prevents him giving clear answers.
options.C: Nerves can be a sign that he cares about doing well.

questions.explanation: He says nerves show the event matters to him, rather than predicting failure.

questions.question: What does Liam consider most important for beginners?

options.C: Studying animal behaviour in a classroom.

questions.explanation: He says a photograph is not worth harming or frightening wildlife.
